design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate sidescrolling levels using Jim's gun and whip mechanics while defeating enemies and bosses; whip also enables swinging from hooks for traversal
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Combat-platforming balance with environmental obstacles; protecting NPCs (Peter Puppy level) adds protective escort tension
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control an absurdist earthworm in a robotic suit battling surreal villains; wield unconventional weapons (organic whip, gun) in a chaotic world
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately satirical and absurdist design philosophy—creators explicitly designed it as satire after years of licensed games with preset limitations. Refuses conventional platformer tropes: surreal boss designs (Queen Slug-for-a-Butt, Bob the Killer Goldfish, Professor Monkey-for-a-Head), bizarre environmental storytelling, and tonal unpredictability. The protagonist is literally an earthworm, not a humanoid hero. Gameplay shifts constantly (racing sections, bungee-jumping, underwater mazes, escort mechanics) refuse genre convention

difficulty.lives_system: Health depletion system; Peter Puppy level punishes player by depleting health when NPC takes damage
difficulty.checkpointing: Levels are discrete units; racing 'Andy Asteroids' interludes gate progression—losing race forces boss fight against Psy-Crow before advancing
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Racing sections create variable difficulty gating: winning bypasses boss fight, losing forces additional encounter. Escort mechanics create skill asymmetry (protecting Peter Puppy requires managing two characters)

progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential level progression with boss encounters culminating most levels; racing interludes (Andy Asteroids) gate advancement with variable outcomes
replayability_hooks: Multiple platform versions with different visual/audio implementations
Varied level mechanics encourage replaying for mastery (whipping hooks, escort sections, underwater navigation)
Boss variety provides distinct mechanical challenges per encounter
Tonal unpredictability and absurdist design reward exploration for comedic/surreal moments

world_building_techniques: Character design satirizes genre conventions (earthworm protagonist, surreal villain roster)
Environmental absurdism: cow-launching mechanic leads to princess's death (subverts expected ending)
NPC personality (Peter Puppy's aggression when harmed) establishes world consistency despite chaos
Level naming and design reinforce comedic tone ('For Pete's Sake', 'Snot a Problem')
Boss name/appearance pairs create surreal worldbuilding (Major Mucus, Evil the Cat, Doc Duodenum as organ antagonist)

boss_design.philosophy: Surreal, variety-focused antagonists that prioritize absurdist humor and mechanical novelty over realistic threat design. Each boss visually embodies its concept without restraint (biological names given mechanical/grotesque forms). Racing boss (Psy-Crow) integrates with level-gating system
notable_bosses: Psy-Crow (recurring racing antagonist, gates progression in Andy Asteroids sections)
Evil the Cat (conceptual simplicity in naming vs. game's complexity)
Bob the Killer Goldfish (genre subversion: aquatic non-threat becomes boss)
Professor Monkey-for-a-Head exclusive Robotic Chicken (design excess)
Chuck (junkyard antagonist with vomit-based attacks, unique appearance)
Doc Duodenum (crazed organ of alien entity—anatomical horror design)

faction_indicators: Princess What's-Her-Name (goal/rescue fantasy, subverted by cow death)
Peter Puppy (ally requiring protection, shows player's capacity for care)
danger_signals: Boss rosters indicate world threat diversity (no unified faction, chaotic antagonism)
Earthworm protagonist signifies vulnerability/underdog status against humanoid villains
Suit dependency (super suit provides agency; its loss would strip Jim of capability)
environmental_storytelling: New Junk City level design suggests post-industrial decay world
Underwater maze indicates ecosystem diversity within game world
Suit origin (falling from sky) suggests external/alien intervention in Jim's world
Bungee-jumping competitive level implies established sports/recreation infrastructure
